skillName: Interior angle sum of any polygon
misconceptionSummary: Students memorise the formula without understanding why subtracting 2 is necessary or how triangles relate to the polygon.

visuals.ruleText: Choose one vertex and draw diagonals to all non-adjacent vertices — this creates triangles
visuals.subText: An n-sided polygon always splits into (n − 2) triangles this way

steps.narration: Pick any vertex of a polygon and draw lines to all other vertices that are not directly next to it. This splits the polygon into triangles. A polygon with n sides produces exactly n minus two triangles — and that is where the formula comes from.

lines.text: From one vertex, you can draw diagonals to (n − 3) other vertices

steps.narration: From one vertex, you can reach n minus three other vertices by diagonal — not the two adjacent ones. That makes n minus three diagonals, creating n minus three plus one equals n minus two triangles. For a pentagon: five minus two equals three triangles.

lines.text: This IS the formula — derived, not memorised  ✓

steps.narration: Each of the n minus two triangles has an angle sum of one hundred and eighty degrees. Multiply: n minus two times one hundred and eighty. The formula emerges directly from counting the triangles — it is not a magic fact to memorise.

visuals.label: The formula counts triangles — subtract 2 because two sides do not create new triangles from the chosen vertex

steps.narration: The formula is derived — not arbitrary. Count the triangles from one vertex, multiply by one hundred and eighty. The n minus two arises naturally from the geometry of diagonals.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using the formula without understanding the (n − 2). It counts the triangles formed by drawing diagonals from one vertex. A pentagon makes 3 triangles (5 − 2), giving 3 × 180° = 540°.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The formula 180(n−2) counts triangles. Draw diagonals from one vertex of an n-sided polygon — you get n−2 triangles. Multiply by 180° because each triangle sums to 180°. The formula is derived from the triangle rule.
